name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ وَهُوَ كُرْهٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَعَسَىٰٓ أَن تُحِبُّوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ شَرٌّ لَّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٢١٦)
```

```english
Warfare has been prescribed for you, though it is repulsive to you. Yet it may be that you dislike something, which is good for you, and it may be that you love something, which is bad for you, and Allah knows and you do not know. (216)
```

```arabic
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ ۖ قُلْ قِتَالٌ فِيهِ كَبِيرٌ ۖ وَصَدٌّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَكُفْرٌۢ بِهِۦ وَٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَإِخْرَاجُ أَهْلِهِۦ مِنْهُ أَكْبَرُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَكْبَرُ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ ۗ وَلَا يَزَالُونَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَرُدُّوكُمْ عَن دِينِكُمْ إِنِ ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يَرْتَدِدْ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ فَيَمُتْ وَهُوَ كَافِرٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢١٧)
```

```english
They ask you concerning warfare in the holy month. Say, ‘It is an outrageous thing to fight in it, but to keep [people] from Allah’s way, and to be unfaithful to Him, and [to keep people from] the Holy Mosque, and to expel its people from it are more outrageous with Allah. And persecution is graver than killing. They will not cease fighting you until they turn you away from your religion, if they can. And whoever of you turns away from his religion and dies faithless—they are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter. They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (217)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢١٨)
```

```english
Indeed those who are faithful and those who have migrated and waged jihad in the way of Allah—it is they who expect Allah’s mercy, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (218)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** كتب عليكم القتال وهو كره لكم (Warfare has been prescribed for you, though it is detestable to you). Prescription (الكتابة), as has been mentioned repeatedly, is apparent in obligation (الفرض) when the discourse is directed toward explaining legislation (التشريع), and in the definitive decree (القضاء الحتم) when it pertains to the creative order (التكوين). Thus, the verse indicates the obligation of fighting upon all the believers (المؤمنين), as the address is directed toward them, except for those excluded by evidence, such as His saying, the Exalted: **لَّيْسَ عَلَى الْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى الْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى الْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ** — "There is no blame on the blind, nor is there any blame on the lame, nor is there any blame on the sick" (24:61), and other verses and proofs.

The subject of "prescribed" (كتب) was not explicitly mentioned because the sentence is appended with His saying: **وَهُوَ كُرْهٌ لَّكُمْ** — "though it is detestable to you." It is not appropriate to mention the subject [Allah] here, in order to safeguard His station from violation and to protect His Name from being treated lightly, lest the prescription explicitly attributed to Him become an object of the believers' detestation.

Detestation (الكره), with a *damma* on the *kaf*, refers to the hardship (المشقة) that a person perceives within himself naturally or otherwise. Detestation (الكره), with a *fatha* on the *kaf*, refers to the hardship imposed upon him from the outside, as if another person forces him to do what he dislikes. The Exalted said: **لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ إِن تَرِثُوا النِّسَاءَ كَرْهًا** — "It is not lawful for you to inherit women against their will" (4:19), and He said: **فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيَا طَوْعًا او كَرْهً** — "Then He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly’" (41:11). The fact that the prescribed fighting was detestable to the believers was either because fighting—involving as it does the destruction of souls, the exhaustion of bodies, financial losses, and the disruption of security, low prices, and prosperity, among other things that a person inevitably dislikes in his social life—was naturally detestable and arduous for the believers. For although Allah, the Glorified, praised the believers in His Book as He did, and mentioned that among them are men truthful in their faith and successful in their striving, He nevertheless rebuked a group of them for the deviation and slipping in their hearts. This is apparent by referring to the verses revealed regarding the battles of Badr, Uhud, al-Khandaq, and others. It is well known that it is permissible to attribute detestation and sluggishness to a people among whom some are detesting and some are not, while the majority of them are detesting; this is one perspective.

Or it may be because the believers were of the view that fighting the disbelievers (الكفار), given their equipment and strength, would not serve the welfare of Islam and the Muslims, and that prudence lay in delaying it until they had completed their preparations by increasing their numbers, wealth, and establishing their capability. Therefore, they detested embarking upon fighting and haste in combat. Thus, the Exalted explained that they were mistaken in this view and opinion, for Allah has a purpose in this matter which He shall bring to pass, and He is the Knower of the reality of the matter while they know only its outward appearance; this is another perspective.

Or it may be because the believers, having been raised by the training of the Quran, had ingrained in them the character of compassion for Allah's creation and the faculty of mercy and kindness. Thus, they detested fighting the disbelievers because it led to the destruction of souls among them in battles while in a state of disbelief. They were not pleased with that; rather, they preferred to treat them with leniency and mix with them through beautiful social conduct and good invitation (الدعوة الحسنة), hoping they might be guided thereby and enter under the banner of faith (الإيمان), thus preserving the souls of the believers from destruction and the souls of the disbelievers from eternal perdition and permanent ruin. Thus, He explained that they were mistaken in this, for Allah—who is the Legislator of the ruling of fighting—knows that the invitation is not effective in those wretched and losing souls, and that many of them would not return to the religion in a way that would benefit them in this world or the Hereafter. In the human society, they are like a diseased limb whose corruption spreads to the rest of the limbs, for which no remedy is effective except that it be severed and cast away; this is also a perspective.

These are the perspectives by which His saying, the Exalted: (**وَهُوَ كُرْهٌ لَّكُمْ**) — "though it is detestable to you" — is explained. However, the first is more appropriate in view of the verses of rebuke alluded to, and the fact that the expression in His saying: كتب عليكم القتال (Warfare has been prescribed for you) is in the passive voice (بصيغة المجهول), according to the reason mentioned previously, supports this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وعسى ان تكرهوا شيئاً وهو خير لكم (it may be that you detest something and it is good for you). It has been mentioned previously that terms like "it may be" (عسى) and "perhaps" (لعل) in His speech, the Exalted, are used in the sense of hope (الترجي). It is not necessary for the attribute of hope to reside in the self of the Speaker; rather, it is sufficient for it to reside in the addressee or in the context of the discourse. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, only says "it may be that such-and-such happens" not because He hopes for it—exalted is He above that—but so that the addressee or the listener may hope for it.

The repetition of "it may be" (عسى) in the verse is because the believers were detesting war and loving peace. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, guided them to their error in both matters. To explain: if it were said, "it may be that you detest something and it is good for you or love something and it is bad for you," the meaning would be that no weight is given to your detestation or your love, for they may both miss the reality. Such a statement is only addressed to one who has made a single error, like one who detests meeting Zayd only. But as for one who has made two errors, such as detesting social interaction and loving isolation, then eloquence (البلاغة) requires that his error be pointed out in both matters together. Thus, it is said to him: "You were not correct in your detestation, nor were you guided in your love; it may be that you detest something and it is good for you, and it may be that you love something and it is bad for you, because you are ignorant and cannot guide yourself to the reality of the matter." Since the believers, along with their detestation of fighting, were lovers of peace—as is also sensed from His saying, the Exalted, previously: "Do you suppose that you shall enter paradise though there has not yet come to you the like of [what befell] those who passed away before you?"—Allah alerted them to the two errors with two independent sentences: "it may be that you detest" and "it may be that you love."

**His saying, the Exalted:** والله يعلم وانتم لا تعلمون (and Allah knows and you do not know). This is a completion of the explanation of their error. For He, the Exalted, proceeded gradually in explaining that, out of kindness to their minds. He first began by suggesting the possibility of their error in their detestation of fighting by His saying: "it may be that you detest." When their minds became balanced by the occurrence of doubt therein and the removal of the state of compound ignorance (الجهل المركب), He returned to them a second time, stating that this ruling which you detested was only legislated by Allah, who is not ignorant of any of the realities of matters, and that what you see is based on your own selves, which know nothing except what Allah has taught them and whose reality He has uncovered. Therefore, you must surrender the matter to Him, the Glorified.

The verse, in its affirmation of knowledge for Him, the Exalted, absolutely, and the negation of knowledge from others absolutely, corresponds to other verses indicating this meaning, such as His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَيْهِ شَيْءٌ** — "Indeed nothing is hidden from Allah" (3:5), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِمَا شَاءَ** — "and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes" (2:255). Some discussion regarding fighting has already preceded in His saying: **وَقَاتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ** — "Fight in the way of Allah" (2:190).

**His saying, the Exalted:** يسألونك عن الشهر الحرام قتال فيه (They ask you about the sacred month, [and] about fighting in it). The verse includes the prohibition of fighting in the sacred month (الشهر الحرام) and its condemnation as a barring from the way of Allah (سبيل الله) and disbelief (كفر). Its inclusion, alongside that, of the fact that the expulsion of the people of the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) from it is greater in the sight of Allah, and that faithlessness (الفتنة) — *the persecution and trial that leads away from faith* — is greater than killing, signals the occurrence of an incident that necessitated the question, and that there was a killing, and that it occurred by mistake, due to His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verses: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا وَجَاهَدُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أُولَٰئِكَ يَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَ اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ** — "Indeed those who believed and those who migrated and waged jihad in the way of Allah, it is they who expect Allah’s mercy, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (2:218). These are circumstantial clues (قرائن) to the occurrence of a killing among the disbelievers by mistake by the believers in a fight that occurred between them in the sacred month, and the disbelievers' criticism of it. In this is a confirmation of what has been reported in the narrations regarding the story of Abdullah ibn Jahsh and his companions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قل قتال فيه كبير وصد عن سبيل الله وكفر به والمسجد الحرام (Say, ‘Fighting in it is a great [sin]; but barring from the way of Allah and defiance of Him and [barring from] the Sacred Mosque). Barring (الصد) is prevention and turning away. What is meant by the way of Allah (سبيل الله) is worship (العبادة) and rituals (النسك), especially the Hajj. It is apparent that the pronoun in "of Him" (به) refers to the "way," so it would be disbelief in action (كفر في العمل) rather than in belief (الاعتقاد). "The Sacred Mosque" (المسجد الحرام) is coordinated with "the way of Allah," meaning barring from the way of Allah and from the Sacred Mosque.

The verse indicates the sanctity of fighting in the sacred month. It has been said that it is abrogated (منسوخة) by His saying, the Exalted: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ** — "kill the polytheists wherever you find them" (9:6), but this is not correct. Some discussion on this has already passed in the commentary on the verses of fighting.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وإِخراج أهله منه أكبر عند الله والفتنة أكبر من القتل (and the expulsion of its people from it is a greater [sin] in the sight of Allah, and faithlessness is a greater [sin] than killing). That is, what the polytheists did by expelling the Messenger of Allah and the believers from the Migrants (المهاجرين)—who are the people of the Sacred Mosque—from it is greater than the fighting. And the faithlessness (الفتنة) by which they tried the believers through intimidation and invitation to disbelief is greater than killing. Thus, the polytheists have no right to criticize the believers when they have done what is greater than that which they criticized. The believers, in what they struck of them, were only hoping for the mercy of Allah, and Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ولا يزالون يقاتلونكم (And they will not cease fighting you) to the end of the verse. "Until" (حتى) is for causation (للتعليل), meaning: in order to turn you back.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ومن يرتدد منكم عن دينه (And whoever of you turns back from his religion) etc. This is a threat to the apostate (المرتد) of the nullification of deeds (حبط العمل) and eternal abiding in the Fire.

## (A Discourse on Nullification)

Nullification (الحبط) is the invalidation of a deed and the loss of its effect. In the Quran, it is only attributed to deeds (العمل), such as His saying, the Exalted: **لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسرِينَ** — "If you ascribe partners [to Allah], your endeavors will surely come to naught and you will surely be among the losers" (39:65).

And His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَشَاقُّوا الرَّسُولَ مِن بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدَىٰ لَن يَضُرُّوا اللَّهَ شَيْئًا وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَلَا تُبْطِلُوا أَعْمَالَكُمْ** — "Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Apostle after guidance has become manifest to them, they will not harm Allah in the least, and He will make their endeavors come to naught. O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle, and do not let your endeavors go to waste" (47:32-33). The end of the verse indicates by contrast that nullification (الحبط) is in the sense of the invalidation of the deed, as is apparent in His saying, the Exalted: **وَحَبِطَ مَا صَنَعُوا فِيهَا وَبَاطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "and what they had wrought there failed, and what they used to do was in vain" (11:16). Close to it is His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَّنثُورًا** — "Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then render them as scattered dust" (25:23).

In short, nullification (الحبط) is the invalidation of the deed and its failure to produce an effect. It has been said that its root is from *al-habat* (الحبط) with a *fatha* on the *ba*, which is when an animal eats excessively so that its belly swells, often leading to its death.

What the Exalted mentioned regarding the effect of nullification is the invalidation of deeds in both this world and the Hereafter together. Thus, nullification has a connection to deeds in terms of their effect on the life of the Hereafter. For faith (الإيمان) makes the life of this world good just as it makes the life of the Hereafter good. The Exalted said: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَيَاةً طَيِّبةً وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do" (16:97). The loss of the disbeliever's effort—especially one who apostatizes to disbelief after faith—and the nullification of his deeds in this world is apparent and beyond doubt. For his heart is not attached to a stable matter, which is Allah, the Glorified, in whom he would rejoice at the time of blessing, find solace at the time of calamity, and to whom he would return at the time of need. The Exalted said: **أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا** — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122). The verse clarifies that the believer has life and light in his actions in this world, while the disbeliever does not. Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ** — "Then whoever follows My guidance will not go astray, nor will he be wretched. But whoever turns away from My remembrance, his indeed shall be a narrow life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection" (20:123-124), where He explains that the disbeliever's livelihood and life in this world is narrow (ضنك), constricted, and exhausting, while by contrast, the believer's livelihood and life is happy, spacious, and wide.

He has gathered all this and indicated the cause of this happiness and wretchedness in His saying, the Exalted: **ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وإِنَّ الْكَافِرِينَ لَا مَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ** — "That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful, and because the faithless have no protector" (47:11).

It has become clear from what we have presented that what is meant by deeds (الأعمال) is all actions by which a person intends the happiness of life, not specifically acts of worship (الأعمال العبادية) and acts of proximity (الأفعال القربية) that the apostate had performed while in a state of faith. Furthermore, nullification (الحبط) occurs in the case of those who have no acts of worship or proximity, such as the disbelievers and hypocrites, as in His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِن تَنصُرُوا اللَّهَ يَنصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَتَعْسًا لَّهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمَالَهُمْ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا مَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَالَهُمْ** — "O you who have faith! If you help Allah, He will help you and make your feet steady. As for the faithless, their lot shall be fall [and ruin], and He will make their endeavors come to naught. That is because they loathed what Allah had sent down, so He made their endeavors come to naught" (47:7-9). And His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ النَّبِيِّينَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَيَقْتُلُونَ الَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْقِسْطِ مِنَ النَّاسِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّاصِرِينَ** — "Those who deny the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly and kill those who call for justice among the people, inform them of a painful punishment. They are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers" (3:21-22), among other verses.

The sum of the verse, like other verses of nullification, is that disbelief and apostasy necessitate the invalidation of the deed from producing an effect on the happiness of life, just as faith necessitates a life in deeds by which they produce their effect on happiness. If a person believes after disbelief, his deeds come to life in their effect on happiness after having been nullified and void. And if he apostatizes after faith, all his deeds die and are nullified, having no effect on worldly or otherworldly happiness. However, this is hoped for him as long as he does not die in apostasy; but if he dies in apostasy, nullification is finalized for him and wretchedness is prescribed for him.

From here, the futility of the dispute regarding whether the deeds of the apostate remain until the time of death and nullification occurs then, or not, becomes apparent.

To clarify: some have held that the apostate's deeds prior to his apostasy remain until the time of death; if he does not return to faith, they are invalidated by nullification at that point. They argued for this using His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَن يَرْتَدِدْ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِ فَيَمُتْ وَهُوَ كَافِرٌ فَأُولَٰئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ** — "And whoever of you turns back from his religion and dies as a faithless one—it is they whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter" (2:217). This is perhaps supported by His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَّنثُورًا** — "Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then render them as scattered dust" (25:23), for the verse explains the state of the disbelievers at death. It follows from this that if he returns to faith, he regains his previous righteous deeds.

Others have held that apostasy nullifies deeds from their root, so they do not return to him even if he believes after apostasy. Yes, he has what he performs of deeds after believing a second time until the time of death. As for the verse, it only included the condition of death because it is in the station of explaining all his deeds and actions that he performed in this world!

By reflecting on what we have mentioned, you will realize that there is no basis for this dispute at all, and that the verse is concerned with explaining the invalidation of all his deeds and actions in terms of their effect on his happiness!

Here is another issue, as if branching from this one, which is the issue of nullification (الإحباط) and expiation (التكفير) — *the cancellation of sins by good deeds*. It is whether deeds nullify one another or not; rather, does a good deed have its ruling and an evil deed its ruling? Yes, good deeds (الحسنات) may expiate evil deeds (السيئات) by the text of the Quran.

Some have held the view of mutual invalidation and nullification between deeds. These have differed among themselves: some say that every subsequent evil deed nullifies the previous good deed, and vice versa. The implication of this is that a person would only have either a good deed alone or an evil deed alone. Others held the view of balancing (الموازنة), which is that the greater is reduced by the amount of the lesser, and the remainder stays free from the contrary. The implication of both views is that a person would only have one type of deed—good or evil—if he has anything at all.

Both are refuted, first, by His saying, the Exalted: **وَآخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ خَلَطُوا عَمَلًا صَالِحًا وَآخَرَ سَيِّئًا عَسَى اللَّهُ أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِن اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ** — "And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins: they have mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil. Maybe Allah will accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (9:102). The verse is apparent in the diversity of deeds and their remaining as they are until repentance from Allah, the Glorified, reaches them, which contradicts mutual nullification in whatever way they conceived it.

Second: He, the Exalted, in the matter of the effect of deeds, followed the course followed by rational people in human society regarding recompense (المجازاة), which is the reward for a good deed separately and the punishment for an evil deed separately, except in some sins that sever the link of mastership and servitude from its root—which is the case for nullification. The verses following this method are many and rich enough to not require citation.

Others have held that all types of deeds are preserved, and every deed has its effect, whether it be good or evil. Yes, a good deed may expiate an evil one, as the Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِن تَتَّقُوا اللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّكُمْ فُرْقَانًا وَيُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّئَاتِكُمْ** — "O you who have faith! If you are God-conscious, He will give you a criterion [to distinguish between right and wrong], and He will absolve you of your misdeeds" (8:29). And the Exalted said: **فَمَن تَعَجَّلَ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ فَلَا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ** — "Then whoever hastens in two days, there is no sin upon him" (2:203). And the Exalted said: **إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبَائِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّئَاتِكُمْ** — "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" (4:31). Rather, some deeds change an evil deed into a good one, as the Exalted said: **إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلًا صَالِحًا فَأُولَٰئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ اللَّهُ سَيِّئَاتِهِمْ حَسَنَاتٍ** — "excepting those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously; for such, Allah will replace their misdeeds with good deeds" (25:70).

Here is another issue, which is like the root for these two issues: the investigation into the time of entitlement to recompense and its locus. It was said: it is the time of the deed. It was said: at the time of death. It was said: in the Hereafter. It was said: at the time of the deed with the condition of finality (الموافاة), meaning that if he does not persist in the state he was in at the time of the deed until death and his final meeting, he does not deserve it, unless Allah knows what his state will end up being and settle upon, so He writes what he deserves at the time of the deed.

The proponents of each view argued with the verses that suited them, for among them are those that suit each of these times according to application. Sometimes they argued with some fabricated rational perspectives.

What ought to be said is: if we follow, in the chapter of reward, punishment, nullification, expiation, and what follows them, the path of the results of deeds (نتائج الأعمال)—as we explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah is not ashamed to strike a similitude even of a gnat or something above it" (2:26)—the implication of that would be that the human soul (النفس), as long as it is attached to the body, is a transforming substance (جوهر متحول) capable of transformation in its essence and in the effects of its essence from the forms that issue from it and by which happy or wretched results and effects subsist. When a good deed issues from it, a spiritual form (صورة معنوية) occurs in its essence necessitating its being characterized by reward. When a sin issues from it, a spiritual form occurs by which the form of punishment subsists. However, since the essence was subject to transformation and change according to the good and evil deeds that occur to it, it was possible for the existing, present form to be invalidated by its changing into another. This is its state until death occurs, whereupon it departs from the body, movement stops, and transformation and its potential are invalidated. At that point, the forms and their effects become fixed for it with a fixity that does not accept transformation or change, except through forgiveness or intercession (الشفاعة) in the manner we explained previously.

Likewise, if we follow, in reward and punishment, the path of recompense (المجازاة) as we explained previously, the state of the human being in terms of acquiring good deeds and sins in relation to divine obligations and the resulting reward and punishment is like his state in terms of obedience and disobedience in social obligations and the resulting praise and blame. Rational people take to praising the obedient and the doer of good, and blaming the disobedient and the doer of evil, as soon as the act issues from its agent. However, they see the praise and blame they recompense him with as capable of change and transformation, because they see the agent as capable of changing and departing from his state of compliance or rebellion. Thus, the attachment of praise and blame to the agent of the act is actual for them upon the realization of the act, but its persistence is contingent upon the non-realization of what contradicts it. As for the fixity of praise and blame and their necessity such that they are never invalidated, that only occurs when his state becomes fixed such that it never changes, by death or the invalidation of potential in life.

From here it is known: that in all the previous views on the mentioned issues, there is a deviation from the truth because they built the investigation on something other than what it ought to be built upon.

And the truth is, first: that reward and punishment attach to the human being in terms of entitlement as soon as the act necessitating it issues, but it is capable of transformation and change afterward, and it only becomes fixed without passing away at death, as we mentioned.

Second: that the nullification of deeds by disbelief and the like is similar to the entitlement to reward; it is realized upon the issuance of the sin and becomes finalized at death.

Third: that nullification, just as it relates to otherworldly deeds, also relates to worldly deeds.

Fourth: that mutual nullification (التحابط) between deeds is false, unlike expiation (التكفير) and the like.

## (A Discourse on the Rulings of Deeds in Terms of Recompense)

Among the rulings of deeds: that among sins are those that nullify the good deeds of this world and the Hereafter, such as apostasy (الارتداد). The Exalted said: **وَمَن يَرْتَدِدْ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِ فَيَمُتْ وَهُوَ كَافِرٌ فَأُولَٰئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ** — "And whoever of you turns back from his religion and dies as a faithless one—it is they whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter" (2:217). And like disbelief in the signs of Allah and stubbornness therein. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ النَّبِيِّينَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَيَقْتُلُونَ الَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْقِسْطِ مِنَ النَّاسِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ** — "Those who deny the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly and kill those who call for justice among the people, inform them of a painful punishment. They are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter" (3:21-22). Likewise, among acts of obedience are those that expiate the sins of this world and the Hereafter, such as Islam and repentance (التوبة). The Exalted said: **قُلْ يَا عِبَادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَا تَقْنَطُوا مِن رَّحْمَةِ اللَّهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعًا إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنِيبُوا إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ وَأَسْلِمُوا لَهُ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذَابُ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ وَاتَّبِعُوا أَحْسَنَ مَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُم** — "Say, ‘[Allah says,] O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. Turn penitently to your Lord and submit to Him before the punishment comes to you, for then you will not be helped. And follow the best of what has been sent down to you from your Lord’" (39:53-55). And the Exalted said: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ** — "Then whoever follows My guidance will not go astray, nor will he be wretched. But whoever turns away from My remembrance, his indeed shall be a narrow life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection" (20:123-124).

Also: among sins are those that nullify some good deeds, such as defying the Messenger. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَشَاقُّوا الرَّسُولَ مِن بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدَىٰ لَن يَضُرُّوا اللَّهَ شَيْئًا وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَلَا تُبْطِلُوا أَعْمَالَكُمْ** — "Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Apostle after guidance has become manifest to them, they will not harm Allah in the least, and He will make their endeavors come to naught. O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle, and do not let your endeavors go to waste" (47:32-33). For the contrast between the two verses requires that the command to obey be in the sense of the prohibition of defiance, and the invalidation of the deed is nullification. And like raising the voice above the voice of the Prophet. The Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَرْفَعُوا أَصْوَاتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ النَّبِيِّ وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا لَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ أَن تَحْبَطَ أَعْمَالُكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ** — "O you who have faith! Do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet, and do not speak aloud to him like you shout to one another, lest your works should fail without your being aware" (49:2).

Likewise, among acts of obedience are those that expiate some sins, such as the prescribed prayers. The Exalted said: **وَأَقِمِ الصَّلَاةَ طَرَفَيِ النَّهَارِ وَزُلَفًا مِّنَ اللَّيْلِ إِنَّ الْحَسَنَاتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئَاتِ** — "Maintain the prayer at the two ends of the day, and during the early hours of the night. Indeed good deeds efface misdeeds" (11:114). And like the Hajj. The Exalted said: **فَمَن تَعَجَّلَ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ فَلَا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ وَمَن تَأَخَّرَ فَلَا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ** — "Then whoever hastens in two days, there is no sin upon him, and whoever tarries, there is no sin upon him" (2:203). And like the avoidance of major sins. The Exalted said: **إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبَائِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّئَاتِكُمْ** — "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" (4:31). And the Exalted said: **الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَالْفَوَاحِشَ إِلَّا اللَّمَمَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ وَاسِعُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ** — "Those who avoid major sins and indecencies, except [minor] faults. Indeed your Lord is expansive in forgiveness" (53:32).

Also: among sins are those that transfer the good deeds of their agent to another, such as killing. The Exalted said: **إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَن تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ** — "I desire that you should bear my sin and your own sin" (5:29). This meaning has been reported regarding backbiting (الغيبة), slander (البهتان), and others in the narrations transmitted from the Prophet and the Imams of the People of the House. Likewise, among acts of obedience are those that transfer sins to another, as will come.

Also: among sins are those that transfer the like of the sins of another to the person, not the sins themselves. The Exalted said: **لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزَارَهُمْ كَامِلَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَمِنْ أَوْزَارِ الَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ** — "that they may bear their burdens in full on the Day of Resurrection, along with some of the burdens of those whom they mislead without any knowledge" (16:25). And He said: **وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقَالَهُمْ وَأَثْقَالًا مَّعَ أَثْقَالِهِمْ** — "They will surely carry their own burdens, and [other] burdens along with their own burdens" (29:13). Likewise, among acts of obedience are those that transfer the like of the good deeds of another to the person, not the deeds themselves. The Exalted said: **وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا وَآثَارَهُمْ** — "and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps" (36:12).

Also: among sins are those that necessitate the doubling of punishment. The Exalted said: **إِذاً لَّأَذَقْنَاكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَيَاةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَمَاتِ** — "then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death" (17:75). And the Exalted said: **يُضَاَعَفْ لَهَا الْعَذَابُ ضِعْفَيْنِ** — "the punishment shall be doubled for her" (33:30). Likewise, among acts of obedience are those that necessitate doubling, such as spending in the way of Allah. The Exalted said: **مَّثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنَابِلَ فِي كُلِّ سُنبُلَةٍ مِّائَةُ حَبَّةٍ** — "The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is that of a grain which grows seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains" (2:261). Similar to it is what is in His saying, the Exalted: **أُولَٰئِكَ يُؤْتَوْنَ أَجْرَهُم مَّرَّتَيْنِ** — "It is they who will be given their reward twice" (28:54). And what is in His saying, the Exalted: **يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِن رَّحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ نُورًا تَمْشُونَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ** — "He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by, and forgive you" (57:28). Furthermore, a good deed is doubled with Allah absolutely. The Exalted said: **مَن جَاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا** — "Whoever brings a virtue shall receive ten times its like" (6:160).

Also: among good deeds are those that change evil deeds into good deeds. The Exalted said: **إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلًا صَالِحًا فَأُولَٰئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ اللَّهُ سَيِّئَاتِهِمْ حَسَنَاتٍ** — "excepting those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously; for such, Allah will replace their misdeeds with good deeds" (25:70).

Also: among good deeds are those that necessitate the attachment of their like to another. The Exalted said: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَانٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَمَا أَلَتْنَاهُم مِّنْ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَيْءٍ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ** — "As for those who have faith and whose descendants followed them in faith, We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint anything of their works. Every person is a hostage to what he has earned" (52:21). It is possible to obtain the like of it in evil deeds, such as the oppression of people's orphans, which necessitates the descent of its like upon the orphans from the oppressor's progeny. The Exalted said: **وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَافًا خَافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ** — "Let those fear who, if they left behind them a helpless offspring, would be afraid on their account" (4:9).

Also: among good deeds are those that repel the evil deeds of their owner to another and attract the good deeds of another to him. Just as among evil deeds are those that repel the good deeds of their owner to another and attract his evil deeds to him. This is among the wondrous matters in the chapter of recompense and entitlement; the investigation into it will come in His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَمِيزَ اللَّهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعًا فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ** — "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and put the bad on one another, and heap them all together, and cast them into hell" (8:37).

Regarding the instances of these verses, there are many diverse narrations; we shall present each of them when discussing the verses that suit them, if Allah, the Mighty, wills.

By reflecting on the previous verses and meditating upon them, it becomes clear: that in deeds, in terms of recompense—that is, in terms of their effect on happiness and wretchedness—there is a system that differs from the system existing between them in terms of their nature in this world. This is because the act of eating, for example, insofar as it is a collection of physical movements, active and passive, only relates to its agent in a way that gives him satiety, for instance, and does not bypass him to another, nor is it transferred from him to another person besides him. Likewise, it relates to the food eaten in a way that entails its transformation from one form to another, for instance, and does not exceed it to another, nor is it replaced by another, nor does it turn away from its identity and essence. Likewise, if Zayd strikes Amr, the specific movement was a striking and nothing else, and Zayd was a striker and nothing else, and Amr was struck and nothing else, among other examples. But these acts, according to the realm of happiness and wretchedness, follow other than these rulings, as the Exalted said: **وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا وَلَٰكِن كَانُوا أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** — "They did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong [only] themselves" (2:57). And the Exalted said: **وَلَا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ** — "and the evil scheme besets only its authors" (35:43). And the Exalted said: **انظُرْ كَيْفَ كَذَبُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ** — "Look, how they lie against themselves" (6:24). And the Exalted said: **ثُمَّ قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ قَالُوا ضَلُّوا عَنَّا بَل لَّمْ نَكُن نَّدْعُو مِن قَبْلُ شَيْئًا كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الْكَافِرِينَ** — "Then they will be asked, ‘Where are those you used to ascribe as partners besides Allah?’ They will say, ‘They have forsaken us. Rather, we did not use to invoke anything before.’ Thus does Allah lead the faithless astray" (40:73-74).

In short: the world of recompense may change the act into something other than itself, may transfer the act and attribute it to other than its agent, and may give the act a ruling other than its own, among other effects that differ from the system of this physical world.

It is not appropriate for a doubter to imagine that this invalidates the proof of the intellects in the case of deeds and their effects and corrupts the rational judgment such that nothing of it remains stable. For we see that Allah, the Glorified and Exalted (in what He recounted in His Book), or His angels entrusted with matters, argue against the criminals at the time of death and the intermediate realm (البرزخ), and likewise in the Resurrection, the Fire, and the Garden, with rational proofs that the intellects recognize. The Exalted said: **وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَن فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَن شَاءَ اللَّهُ ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرَىٰ فَإِذَا هُمْ قِيَامٌ يَنظُرُونَ وَأَشْرَقَتِ الْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّهَا وَوُضِعَ الْكِتَابُ وَجِيءَ بِالنَّبِيِّينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُم بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ** — "And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon, except those whom Allah wishes. Then it will be blown another time, and behold, they will be standing, looking on. And the earth will get bright with the light of its Lord, and the Book will be set up, and the prophets and the witnesses will be brought, and judgment will be made between them with justice, and they will not be wronged. Every soul will be paid in full for what it has done, and He knows best what they do" (39:68-70). It has been repeated in the Quran the report that Allah will judge between people with truth on the Day of Resurrection regarding that in which they used to differ. Sufficient in this chapter is what Allah recounted from Satan in His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَ الشَّيْطَانُ لَمَّا قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ وَعدَكُمْ وَعْدَ الْحَقِّ وَوَعَدتُّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُكُمْ وَمَا كَانَ لِيَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَانٍ إِلَّا أَن دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي فَلَا تَلُومُونِي وَلُومُوا أَنفُسَكُم** — "When the matter is decided, Satan will say, ‘Indeed Allah made you a true promise. I too made you promises, but I failed you. I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me. So do not blame me, but blame yourselves’" (14:22).

From here we know: that the proof of the intellects is not invalidated in the realm of deeds and the abode of recompense, despite the clear difference between the two realms—that is, the realm of nature and the realm of recompense—as we have pointed out.

What resolves this knot is: that Allah spoke with people in inviting and guiding them in the language of their own selves, and followed in His addresses to them and His explanations for them the course of social intellects (العقول الاجتماعية). He held to the principles and laws circulating in the world of servitude and mastership. Thus, He considered Himself a Master, the people as servants, and the prophets as messengers to them. He reached them with command and prohibition, urging and restraining, glad tidings and warning, promise and threat, and all that follows this path of punishment, forgiveness, and other things.

This is the method of the Noble Quran in its speaking to people. It declares that the matter is greater than what people imagine or what occurs to their fancy, but it is something that their capacities cannot contain and realities that their understandings cannot encompass. Therefore, it descended to a level close to the horizon of their perception so that they might attain what Allah willed them to attain of the interpretation (تأويل) of this Mighty Book, as the Exalted said: **وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** — "By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed in the Mother Book with Us, exalted and full of wisdom" (43:2-4).

Thus, the Noble Quran relies, in the specifics of what it has announced regarding the rulings of recompense and what is connected to them, on the universal rational rulings (الأحكام الكلية العقلائية) circulating among the rational, which are built upon benefits and harms. Among the subtle matters is: that these realities hidden from the surface of ordinary understandings are capable of application to the mentioned rational rulings and possible to be explained by them. For the social practical intellect (العقل العملي الاجتماعي) does not refuse, for example: being severe with some corrupters by holding them accountable for all the social harms and corruptions resulting from their action, as if the killer were held accountable for all the social rights lost due to the death of the killed, or as if one who established an evil custom were held accountable for all the violations occurring according to his custom. In the first example, it judges that the sins which were seen outwardly as acts of the killed, their agent is the killer according to rational convention (الاعتبار العقلائي). In the second example, that the evil deeds performed by the followers of that evil custom are acts performed by the first who established that followed custom, while they are simultaneously acts of the followers therein; they are acts for both of them together. Therefore, he is held accountable for them just as they are held accountable.

Likewise, it can judge that the agent of an act is other than its agent, or that a specific, limited act is other than that act, or that the good deeds of another are good deeds for the person, or that the person has the like of those good deeds—all of that by the requirement of the existing benefits.

Thus, the Noble Quran explains these wondrous rulings existing in recompense—such as the recompense of a person for the act of another, whether good or evil, the attribution of the act to other than its agent, and making the act other than itself, among others—and clarifies them by the rational laws existing in the sphere of society and on the surface of general understandings, even if they were, according to reality, of a system other than the system of the senses. The social rational rulings were confined and restricted to the life of this world, and what is hidden from the person today will be uncovered for him on the day when the secrets are tested, as the Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ جِئْنَاهُم بِكِتَابٍ فَصَّلْنَاهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُ يَوْمَ يَأْتِي تَأْوِيلُهُ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِالْحَقِّ** — "Certainly We have brought them a Book, which We have elaborated with knowledge, as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. Do they await anything but its interpretation? The day its interpretation comes, those who had forgotten it before will say, ‘Our Lord’s apostles had certainly brought the truth’" (7:52-53). And the Exalted said: **وَمَا كَانَ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنُ أَن يُفْتَرَىٰ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلَٰكِن تَصْدِيقَ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ الْكِتَابِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِن رَّبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** — "This Quran could not have been fabricated by anyone besides Allah; rather, it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of the Book, there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds" (10:37), until He said: **بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ** — "Rather, they deny that whose knowledge they do not comprehend, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them" (10:39).

By this which we have mentioned, the apparent contradiction is removed between these verses containing these wondrous rulings and the like of His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُ وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ** — "So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it" (99:7-8). And His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ** — "and no bearer shall bear another’s burden" (6:164). And His saying, the Exalted: **كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ** — "Every person is a hostage to what he has earned" (52:21). And His saying, the Exalted: **وأَن لَّيْسَ لِلْإِنسَانِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَىٰ** — "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for" (53:39). And His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَظْلِمُ النَّاسَ شَيْئًا** — "Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least" (10:44), among many other verses.

This is because the previous verses judge that the sins of the oppressed killed were only performed by the oppressive killer, so his being held accountable is for his own act, not for the act of another. Likewise, they judge that whoever followed an evil custom and performed a sin based on that following, did not perform it alone; rather, he performed it and the one followed performed it. Thus, the sin is two sins. Likewise, they judge that whoever helped an oppressor in his oppression or followed an imam of misguidance is a partner in his sin and an agent like him. These and their like are among the instances of His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ** — "and no bearer shall bear another’s burden" — and its counterparts in terms of recompense, not that they are excluded from its ruling by exception or by violation.

To this points His saying, the Exalted: **وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُم بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ** — "and judgment will be made between them with justice, and they will not be wronged. Every soul will be paid in full for what it has done, and He knows best what they do" (39:69-70). His saying: "and He knows best what they do," indicates or suggests that the paying in full of every soul for what it has done is only according to what Allah, the Glorified, knows and calculates of their acts, not according to what they calculate from themselves without knowledge or intellect. For Allah has stripped them of intellect in this world where He said, the Exalted, recounting from the companions of the Blaze: **لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَو نَعْقِلُ مَا كُنَّا فِي أَصْحَابِ السَّعِيرِ** — "Had we listened or exercised our reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze" (67:10). And in the Hereafter also, where He said: **وَمَن كَانَ فِي هَٰذِهِ أَعْمَىٰ فَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ أَعْمَىٰ وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا** — "But whoever is blind in this [world] will be blind in the Hereafter [as well], and still more astray from the way" (17:72). And the Exalted said in confirming this stripping: **نَارُ اللَّهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ** — "[It is] the fire of Allah, set ablaze, which overspreads the hearts" (104:6-7). And He said, the Exalted, in confirming this stripping: **قَالَتْ أُخْرَاهُمْ لِأُولَاهُمْ رَبَّنَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ أَضَلُّونَا فَآتِهِمْ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا مِّنَ النَّارِ قَالَ لِكُلٍّ ضِعْفٌ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تَعْلَمُونَ** — "The last of them will say about the first of them, ‘Our Lord, it was they who led us astray; so give them a double punishment of the Fire.’ He will say, ‘For each there is a double [punishment], but you do not know’" (7:38). Thus, He affirmed for both the followed and their followers a double punishment: as for the followed, for their misguidance and their misleading others; and as for the followers, for their misguidance and their upholding the matter of those they followed by following them. Then He mentioned that they all do not know.

**If you say:** The appearance of these verses that strip knowledge from the criminals in this world and the Hereafter contradicts other verses that affirm knowledge for them, such as His saying, the Exalted: **كِتَابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آيَاتُهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ** — "a Book whose signs have been elaborated, an Arabic Quran, for a people who have knowledge" (41:3). And like the verses that argue against them, for there is no meaning in arguing against one who has no knowledge or understanding of the proof. Furthermore, these very verses include types of arguments against them in the Hereafter, and there is no escape from affirming intellect and perception for them therein. Moreover, there are verses here that affirm knowledge and certainty for them specifically in the Hereafter, such as His saying, the Exalted: **لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ** — "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have suspended your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today" (50:22). And His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ تَرَىٰ إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ نَاكِسُو رُءُوسِهِمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنَا أَبْصَرْنَا وَسَمِعْنَا فَارْجِعْنَا نَعْمَلْ صَالِحًا إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ** — "Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [confessing], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard. Send us back so that we may act righteously. Indeed we are [now] convinced’" (32:12).

**I say:** The reference of the negation of knowledge from them in this world is the negation of following what they have of knowledge. And the meaning of its negation from them in the Hereafter is the necessity of what they followed of ignorance in this world for them at the time of Resurrection and the non-separation of deeds from them, as the Exalted said: **وَكُلَّ إِنسَانٍ أَلْزَمْنَاهُ طَائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ كِتَابًا يَلْقَاهُ مَنشُورًا** — "We have fastened every man’s bird of omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open" (17:13). And His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَ يَا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ** — "he will say, ‘I wish there were between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion!’" (43:38), among other verses. The full treatment of this investigation will come in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ آيَاتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** — "Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may exercise your reason" (2:242).

Imam al-Ghazali answered the problem of the transfer of deeds with another answer he mentioned in some of his treatises, saying in summary: The transfer of good and evil deeds due to oppression occurs in this world at the time the oppression takes place, but it is uncovered on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, the oppressor, for example, sees his own acts of obedience in the register of another. They were not transferred at that time, but in this world, as the Exalted said: **لِّمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** — "To whom does the sovereignty belong today? To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!" (40:16). He reported the fixity of sovereignty for Him, the Exalted, in the Hereafter, while it did not originate for Him, the Exalted, there; rather, He is always the Sovereign, except that its reality is not uncovered to all creatures except on the Day of Resurrection. What a person does not know is not existent for him, even if it is existent in itself. So when he knows it, it becomes existent for him as if it were found now in his regard.

Thus, the saying of those who said, "How can the non-existent be transferred, and how can the accident (العرض) be transferred?" has fallen. We say: What is transferred is the reward of obedience, not the obedience itself. But since obedience is intended for its reward, the transfer of its effect was expressed as the transfer of itself. The effect of obedience is not something external to the person that attaches to him, such that it would be problematic that its transfer in this world is of the type of the impossible transfer of an accident, and its transfer in the Hereafter after its non-existence is of the type of the impossible restoration of the non-existent. If it were a substance (جوهر), then what is this substance?! Rather, what is meant by the effect of obedience is its effect in the heart by illumination (التنوير). For acts of obedience have an effect in the heart by illumination, and sins have an effect in it by hardness and darkness. By the lights of obedience, the heart's affinity with the world of light, gnosis, and witnessing is strengthened; and by oppression and hardness, the heart becomes prepared for the veil and distance. Between the effects of acts of obedience and sins, there is succession and opposition, as the Exalted said: **إِن الْحَسَنَاتِ يُذْهِبْنَ السَّيِّئَاتِ** — "Indeed good deeds efface misdeeds" (11:114). And the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Follow the evil deed with a good deed, it will erase it." Sins are purifications for offenses; therefore, he (عليه السلام) said: "A man is rewarded even for the thorn that pricks his foot," and he (عليه السلام) said: "The legal punishments (الحدود) are expiations (كفارات)."

So the oppressor follows his oppression with darkness in his heart and hardness that necessitates the erasure of the effect of light that was in his heart from the acts of obedience he had performed. The oppressed person suffers, so his desire is broken, and the effect of the sins that caused darkness in his heart is erased from his heart, so his heart becomes illuminated with a type of illumination. Thus, what was in the heart of the oppressor of light has moved to the heart of the oppressed, and what was in the heart of the oppressed of darkness has moved to the heart of the oppressor. This is the meaning of the transfer of good and evil deeds.

If someone says: This is not a real transfer, since its result is the invalidation of light from the heart of the oppressor and the occurrence of another light in the heart of the oppressed, and the invalidation of darkness from the heart of the oppressed and the occurrence of another darkness in the heart of the oppressor, and this is not a transfer in reality.

We say: The term "transfer" (النقل) may be applied to such a matter by way of metaphor (الاستعارة), just as it is said: "The shadow transferred from one place to another," or "The light of the sun or the lamp transferred from the ground to the wall," and so forth. This is the meaning of the "transfer of acts of obedience" (نقل الطاعات); it is nothing more than using "obedience" as a metonymy (كني) for its reward (الثواب), just as the cause (السبب) is used as a metonymy for the effect (المسبب). Thus, the affirmation of a quality in one locus and the nullification of its like in another locus is called "transfer." All of this is common in the language and known by rational proof (البرهان) even if the Law (الشرع) had not mentioned it—so how much more so when it has? (End of summary).

**I say:** The gist of what he provided is that applying the term "transfer" to how Allah, the Glorified, deals with the rights of the killer and the killed is a metaphor within a metaphor. That is: the metaphor of using the name "obedience" for the effect of obedience in the heart, and the metaphor of using the name "transfer" for the erasure of something and the affirmation of another in a different locus. If this approach were applied consistently to all the mentioned rulings of deeds, all these rulings would become figurative (مجازات). However, you have already learned that He, the Glorified, established these rulings based on what the social practical intellect (العقل العملي الاجتماعي) perceives and upon which it builds its judgments regarding benefits (المصالح) and harms (المفاسد). There is no doubt that these intellectual judgments are issued by the intellect with the conviction of reality (الحقيقة). Thus, the killer, for example, is held accountable for the crime of the killed, or the killed (or his heirs) is gifted with the good deed of the killer, and the like, based on the conviction that the crime is the very same crime and the good deed is the very same good deed, and so on.

This is the state of these rulings within the context of society, which is the domain of the judgments of the practical intellect (العقل العملي). As for any context other than this—namely, the domain of realities (الحقائق)—then all of them are figurative (مجازات) except by way of analysis. This means that since these very concepts are conventional concepts (مفاهيم اعتبارية) derived from realities taken by way of claim and comparison, they are all figurative when measured against those realities from which they were derived. So understand that.

**Among the rulings of deeds:** is that they are preserved, recorded, and embodied (متجسمة), as the Exalted said: **يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُّحْضَرًا وَمَا عَمِلَتْ مِن سُوءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَهُ أَمَدًا بَعِيدًا** — "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil, it has done, it will wish there were a wide gulf between it and that [day]" (3:30). And the Exalted said: **وَكُلَّ إِنسَانٍ أَلْزَمْنَاهُ طَائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ كِتَابًا يَلْقَاهُ مَنشُورًا** — "We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open" (17:13). And the Exalted said: **وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا وَآثَارَهُمْ وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَاهُ فِي إِمَامٍ مُّبِين** — "Indeed it is We who revive the dead and write what they have sent ahead and their effects [which they left behind], and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam" (36:12). And the Exalted said: **لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ** — "[He will be told,] ‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today’" (50:22). The discussion regarding the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال) has already passed.

**Among the rulings of deeds:** is that there is a connection between them and external events. By "deeds," we mean the good deeds (الحسنات) and evil deeds (السيئات) which are the titles of external movements, rather than the motions and rests that are the effects of natural bodies. For the Exalted said: **وَمَا أَصَابَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُو عَن كَثِيرٍ** — "Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]" (42:30). And the Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُغَيِّرُ مَا بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ وَإِذَا أَرَادَ اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوءًا فَلَا مَرَدَّ لَهُ** — "Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot unless they change what is in their souls. And when Allah wishes to visit ill on a people, there is nothing that can avert it" (13:11). And the Exalted said: **ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّرًا نِّعْمَةً أَنْعَمَهَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ** — "That is because Allah never changes a blessing He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls" (8:53). These verses are apparent in that there is a certain connection between deeds and events, whether evil or good.

The entirety of the matter is summarized by two verses from the Book of Allah, the Exalted: His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرَىٰ آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَاتٍ مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَٰكِن كَذَّبُوا فَأَخَذْنَاهُم بِمَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ** — "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied; so We seized them because of what they used to earn" (7:96); and His saying, the Exalted: **ظَهَرَ الْفَسَادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** — "Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have wrought, that He may let them taste something of what they have done, so that they may come back" (30:41).

Thus, cosmic events follow deeds to some extent. If the human species proceeds upon obedience to Allah, the Glorified, and follows the path He approves, it entails the descent of good things and the opening of the doors of blessings. Conversely, the deviation of this species from the path of servitude (صراط العبودية), its persistence in transgression and misguidance, the corruption of intentions, and the vileness of deeds necessitate the appearance of corruption in land and sea. This leads to the destruction of nations through the spread of injustice, the loss of security, the emergence of wars, and other evils pertaining to man and his actions. It also leads to the appearance of afflictions and destructive cosmic events such as floods, earthquakes, lightning bolts, tempests, and the like. Allah, the Glorified, has counted the flood of the Arim (سيل العرم), the flood of Nuh, the lightning bolt of Thamud, and the roaring wind of ‘Ad as being of this nature.

Therefore, when a wicked nation (الأمة الطالحة) becomes submerged in vices and sins, Allah makes it taste the evil consequence of its affair, which leads to its destruction and annihilation. The Exalted said: **أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ كَانُوا مِن قَبْلِهِمْ كَانُوا هُمْ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَآثَارًا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ** — "Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? They were greater than them in might and with respect to the effects [they left] in the land. But Allah seized them for their sins, and they had no defender against Allah" (40:21). And the Exalted said: **وَإِذَا أَرَدْنَا أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا فِيهَا فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَاهَا تَدْمِيرًا** — "And when We desire to destroy a town, We command its affluent ones [to obey Us]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly" (17:16). And the Exalted said: **ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا تَتْرَىٰ كُلَّ مَا جَاءَ أُمَّةً رَّسُولُهَا كَذَّبُوهُ فَأَتْبَعْنَا بَعْضَهُم بَعْضًا وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ فَبُعْدًا لِّقَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "Then We sent Our apostles successively. Whenever there came to a nation its apostle, they would impugn him, so We made them follow one another [to destruction] and We made them [the subject of] stories. So away with the people who do not have faith!" (23:44). All of this pertains to the wicked nation; the righteous nation (الأمة الصالحة) is the opposite of that.

The individual, like the nation, is taken to account for good and evil deeds, and for punishments and exemplary penalties (المثلات). However, an individual might be blessed with the blessings of his ancestors, just as he might be taken to account for the injustices of others, such as his fathers and grandfathers. The Exalted said, narrating from Yusuf (peace be upon him): **قَالَ أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهَٰذَا أَخِي قَدْ مَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا إِنَّهُ مَن يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فإِن اللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "He said, ‘I am Joseph, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly shown us favour. Indeed if one is God-conscious and patient, Allah does not let the reward of the virtuous go to waste’" (12:90). What is meant here is the kingdom, honor, and other things Allah bestowed upon him. And the Exalted said: **فَخَسَفْنَا بِهِ وَبِدَارِهِ الْأَرْضَ** — "So We caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling" (28:81). And the Exalted said: **وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا** — "and We assigned them a high and true renown" (19:50), which refers to righteous and blessed progeny, as the Exalted said: **جَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةً بَاقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ** — "and he made it a lasting word among his posterity" (43:28). And the Exalted said: **وَأَمَّا الْجِدَارُ فَكَانَ لِغُلَامَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَالِحًا فَأَرَادَ رَبُّكَ إِن يَبْلُغَا أَشُدَّهُمَا وَيَسْتَخْرِجَا كَنزَهُمَا** — "As for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and there was a treasure under it belonging to them. Their father had been a righteous man. So your Lord desired that they should come of age and take out their treasure" (18:82). And the Exalted said: **وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَافًا خَافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ** — "Let those fear who, if they left behind them a helpless offspring, would be afraid on their account" (4:9). What is meant by this is the oppressed descendant who is afflicted by the injustice of his predecessor.

In short, when Allah pours a blessing upon a nation or an individual, if the recipient is righteous, it is a blessing He has bestowed upon him and a trial (امتحاناً) by which He tests him, as Allah the Exalted narrated from Sulayman when he said: **قَالَ هَٰذَا مِن فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ وَمَن شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَن كَفَرَ فإِنَّ رَبِّي غَنِيٌّ كَرِيمٌ** — "He said, ‘This is by the grace of my Lord, to test me whether I will give thanks or be ungrateful. Whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful [should know that] my Lord is indeed all-sufficient, all-generous’" (27:40). And the Exalted said: **لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِن كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذَابِي لَشَدِيدٌ** — "‘If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe’" (14:7). This verse, like the one before it, indicates that gratitude (الشكر) itself is among the righteous deeds that entail blessings.

If the recipient is wicked, the blessing is a ruse (مكراً) against him, a gradual leading to ruin (استدراجاً), and a granting of respite (إملائاً), as the Exalted said: **وَيَمْكُرُونَ وَيَمْكُرُ اللَّهُ وَاللَّهُ خَيْرُ الْمَاكِرِينَ** — "They were plotting and Allah was plotting, and Allah is the best of plotters" (8:30). And the Exalted said: **سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ** — "We will draw them on [to ruin] degree by degree, in ways they do not know. I will grant them respite; indeed My stratagem is profound" (68:44-45). And the Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ** — "Certainly We tested the people of Pharaoh before them" (44:17).

When calamities descend and afflictions and trials strike a people or an individual, if the afflicted is righteous, it is a trial (فتنة) and an ordeal (محنة) by which Allah tests His servants to distinguish the corrupt from the good. His likeness with the affliction is like gold with the crucible and the touchstone. The Exalted said: **أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَن يُتْرَكُوا أَن يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَهُمْ لَا يُفْتَنُونَ وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْكَاذِبِينَ أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئَاتِ أَن يَسْبِقُونَا سَاءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ** — "Do the people suppose that they will be let off because they say, ‘We believe,’ and they will not be tested? Certainly We tested those who were before them. So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful, and He shall surely ascertain the liars. Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that they can outstrip Us? Evil is the judgment that they make!" (29:2-4). And the Exalted said: **وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنكُمْ شُهَدَاءَ** — "We make such days alternate among the people, so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take martyrs from among you" (3:140).

If the afflicted is wicked, it is a seizure by vengeance (أخذاً بالنقمة) and a punishment for deeds. The previous verses indicate this.

This is the ruling of the deed: it manifests in the cosmos and returns to its doer. As for His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْلَا أَن يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً لَّجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمَٰنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَمَعَارِجَ عَلَيْهَا يَظْهَرُونَ وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْوَابًا وَسُرُرًا عَلَيْهَا يَتَّكِئُونَ وَزُخْرُفًا وإِن كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَّا مَتَاعُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةُ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ** — "Were it not that mankind would have become a single community, We would have surely provided for those who disbelieve in the All-beneficent, silver roofs for their houses and [silver] stairs by which they ascend, and [silver] doors for their houses and [silver] couches on which they recline, and ornaments. Yet all that is nothing but the wares of the life of this world, and the Hereafter with your Lord is for the God-conscious" (43:33-35)—this does not look toward this door. Rather, what is meant (and Allah knows best) is the condemnation of the world and its wares, and that it and its wares have no value before Allah, the Glorified. For this reason, He favors the disbeliever with it. Indeed, value belongs to the Hereafter, and were it not that human individuals are similar and their efforts are uniform and alike, Allah would have restricted it to the disbeliever.

**If it is said:** General and specific events such as floods, earthquakes, contagious diseases, wars, and droughts have constant natural causes (علل طبيعية). When these causes are realized, their effects are realized, whether the souls are righteous or wicked. Therefore, there is no place for attributing them to good and evil deeds; rather, it is a religious hypothesis and a supposition that does not correspond to reality.

**I say:** This is a philosophical objection that does not contradict our current exegetical discussion regarding what is benefited from His speech, the Exalted. We shall address it in detail in a separate philosophical discussion in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرَىٰ آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَاتٍ مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ** — "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven" (7:96).

The summary of the matter is: the doubt arises from a misunderstanding and a failure to notice the objectives of the Quran and its people. They do not mean by their saying, "deeds, whether good or evil, entail corresponding events of good or evil," the nullification of natural causes or the denial of their influence, nor the partnership of deeds with material factors. Just as the monotheists (الإلهيين), by affirming the Maker (الصانع), do not mean to nullify the general law of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية) or to affirm chance and haphazardness in existence, or to make the Maker a partner with natural causes such that some matters are attributed to Him and others to them. Rather, their intention is to affirm a cause in the longitudinal dimension (في طول) of another cause, and a spiritual factor above the material factors, and to attribute the influence to both causes, but in order: primary and secondary, similar to writing being attributed to both the human and his hand.

The core of the discourse is that the driver of creation (سائق التكوين) drives man toward his existential happiness (سعادته الوجودية) and vital perfection (كماله الحيوي), as discussed in the section on general prophethood (النبوة العامة). It is known that among the stages of this species in its journey toward happiness is the stage of deeds. Thus, if an obstacle arises in this journey that necessitates its cessation or brings its traveler to the brink of destruction and ruin, it is met with that which repels the mentioned obstacle or destroys the corrupt part. This is similar to the bodily temperament (المزاج البدني) opposing an ailment that afflicts the body or one of its organs; if it succeeds, it repairs the site, and if it fails, it leaves it paralyzed and useless.

Observation and experience have shown that creation and the cosmic process (الصنع والتكوين) have equipped every specific existent with that which repels the harms and corruptions directed toward it. There is no meaning in excluding man, in his species or as an individual, from this universality! They also show that creation opposes every specific existent with incompatible matters that call it to exercise its existential powers so that it may perfect its existence and reach its goal and happiness which was prepared for it. So why would man not be cared for in this regard?

This is what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَاعِبِينَ مَا خَلَقْنَاهُمَا إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with consummate wisdom, but most of them do not know" (44:38-39); and His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** — "We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain.
